blurb: The classic fragrant cottage-garden wallflower — jewel-toned spring spires of clove-scented bloom. Grown as a biennial on the Front Range.
seasons: Low evergreen rosette; intensely fragrant gold/orange/red/mahogany flowers mid–late spring
wildlife: Fragrant flowers draw bees & early butterflies
toxic: Foliage & seeds contain cardiac glycosides — not for eating
origin: Southern Europe — Mediterranean coasts and rocky hillsides
habitat: Rocky coastal cliffs, old walls, and dry Mediterranean scrub

care.hardiness: Erysimum cheiri is hardy only to about USDA zone 7–9, so it is not a dependable perennial through Boulder's 5b–6a winters — and that's why it's grown as a biennial here: sow one year, bloom and enjoy the next spring, then let it go. A few plants may overwinter in a sheltered, sharply-drained spot, but don't count on long-term survival.[1][2]
care.planting: Set out young plants in early fall or in spring after the mid-May frost, into full sun and fast-draining soil; fall-set plants make a rosette, overwinter (with luck and good drainage), and bloom the following spring. In containers it grows well but potted roots are tender — overwinter the pot in an unheated garage or cold frame, kept on the dry side. Sharp winter drainage matters everywhere; it rots in cold, wet clay.[1][2]
care.sow: Grown as a biennial: sow seed in early-to-mid summer (roughly June–July) for bloom the following spring. Start in flats or a nursery bed, then transplant the young rosettes to their flowering positions by early fall. Seed sown very early spring under cover can occasionally flower the same year as an annual.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun — 6+ hours; it needs the light to bloom heavily and stay compact. A little afternoon shade is tolerated in the hottest sites but full sun is best on the Front Range.[1][2]
care.soil: Lean, well-drained, neutral-to-alkaline soil — Boulder's alkaline ground suits it. It thrives in poor, gritty, even rocky soil and dislikes rich or soggy ground. Improve heavy clay drainage with grit.[1][2]
care.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once rooted. Water to establish, then let it dry between waterings and avoid winter wet, which rots the crown.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space about 12 in apart (up to ~18 in for larger strains); plants reach roughly 1–2 ft tall and 1–1.5 ft wide.[1][2]
care.propagation: From seed (the practical route for this biennial — see Sowing). It self-sows where happy, so leaving a few seed heads can perpetuate a patch; deadheading prevents that. Named perennial cultivars are taken from cuttings, but the species E. cheiri is grown from seed.[1][2]
care.bloom: Intensely clove-fragrant flowers in jewel tones — gold, orange, red, mahogany, and purple — crowd short spikes in mid-to-late spring (about April–June). Bloom is a single glorious spring season, after which biennial plants decline.[1][2]
care.feeding: Little needed; it flowers best in lean soil. A light spring feed or compost is plenty. Excess nitrogen gives leaves at the expense of bloom.[2]
care.maintenance: Deadhead spent spikes to tidy plants and extend bloom (or to prevent self-sowing); leave a few heads if you want volunteer seedlings. Pull and compost the plants after they finish, since they're grown as biennials, and start a fresh sowing for next year.[1][2]
care.troubles: Few serious pests; the main risks are crown rot in wet or poorly-drained soil and short life in cold winters. Sharp drainage and full sun head off most problems. Like other brassicas it can attract flea beetles and the usual cabbage-family pests, but rarely seriously.[1][2]
care.companions: Fragrant and an early-season draw for bees and butterflies — classic in cottage-style spring beds with tulips and other bulbs, and good with other sun-loving, well-drained companions that share its lean-soil, dry-winter preferences.[1][2]

edible.summary: A fragrant ornamental wallflower grown for spring color and scent — not a food plant; do not eat any part.[1][3]
edible.caution: English wallflower is not edible: its foliage and seeds contain cardiac glycosides (cardenolides) — heart-active compounds the plant makes as a chemical defense — so no part should be eaten despite the sweet floral fragrance. Grow it for the bees and the scent, not for consumption.[3]
